section: Q1 algorithm transfer: sum to product

examples.label: Example A: simple formula (a + b)
Example B: loop accumulator (sum)

prompt: Following the loop accumulator pattern, write who_am_i that sets mystery to the PRODUCT of numbers[0..4]. Start mystery at 1 and multiply each element.

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: max to min

examples.label: Example A: find max of numbers[0..4]

prompt: Adapt the max-finder pattern: write who_am_i that finds the MINIMUM of numbers[0..4]. Flip the comparison operator.

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: count threshold change

examples.label: Example A: count positives (> 0)

prompt: Adapt the count pattern: write who_am_i that counts how many of numbers[0..4] are GREATER THAN 10.

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: conditional accumulator

examples.label: Example A: sum all elements
Example B: count positives

prompt: Combine both patterns: write who_am_i that sums ONLY the positive numbers in numbers[0..4]. Use if to guard the accumulator.

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: sum to average

examples.label: Example A: sum pattern (body only)
Example B: average = sum / count (body only)

prompt: Write the full who_am_i function (with signature) that computes the average of numbers[0..4].

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: difference to range

examples.label: Example A: absolute difference of two values

prompt: Adapt: write who_am_i that sets mystery to the RANGE of numbers[0..4] (max minus min). Find max and min first with a loop, then subtract.

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: array size change

examples.label: Example A: sum of 5 elements

prompt: Same sum pattern but with 10 elements: write who_am_i that sums numbers[0..9].

section: Q1 algorithm transfer: count to conditional sum

examples.label: Example A: count negatives

prompt: Adapt the count-negatives pattern: write who_am_i that SUMS only the negative numbers in numbers[0..4]. Replace +1 with +data.numbers[i].

expectedAnswer: printf("%d %s %.2f\n", list[i].year, list[i].make.c_str(), list[i].price);

Level 2: scalar by reference (alias)

section: Q4 progression: print simple to printf struct loop

examples.label: Level 1: print one value with cout
examples.code: cout << x;

examples.label: Level 2: print with printf format
examples.code: printf("%d\n", x);

examples.label: Level 3: print struct field
examples.code: printf("%d\n", p.id);

prompt: Level 4: write a for loop that prints list[i].id (int) and list[i].name (string) for count items using printf.

section: Q3 progression: cin simple to read function

examples.label: Level 1: read one int
examples.code:
int x;
cin >> x;

examples.label: Level 2: read into struct field
examples.code: cin >> p.name;

examples.label: Level 3: read into array element
examples.code: cin >> list[i].name;

prompt: Level 4: write a full Q3 read function for city_data with fields: int population, string name.
